steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The goal is to explain why potassium is not fully liquid in chain melt. The notes provide the mechanism: guest atoms are disordered (liquid-like) but confined to one-dimensional movement by the solid host lattice, whereas full liquidity requires free three-dimensional movement.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'involves a dual structure of a solid host lattice surrounding partially liquid chains': describes the structure of chain melt but does not explain why the guest is not fully liquid. ✗
  • Choice B — 'some atoms exhibit liquid-like disorder but remain confined by a solid lattice, preventing free three-dimensional movement': directly states the mechanism — confinement to one-dimensional movement prevents the free three-dimensional movement that defines a fully liquid state. ✓
  • Choice C — 'atoms enter chain melt, a transitional phase between solid and liquid': contradicts the notes, which state chain melt was determined to be a stable, distinct state — not a transitional phase. ✗
  • Choice D — 'atoms remain disordered in chain melt even under extreme heat, so they cannot reach a fully liquid state': disorder is a property of liquids, not a reason something fails to be liquid; this confuses cause and effect. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
